name: Two Sum
statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
hint: Use a hash map to store visited elements and check if complement exists.
solution: Use a hash map. For each element, check if (target - element) exists in the map. If yes, return indices. Otherwise, add current element to map.

name: Contains Duplicate
statement: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.
hint: Use a HashSet to track seen elements.
solution: Insert elements into a HashSet. If insertion fails (element already exists), return true. Otherwise return false after processing all elements.

name: Majority Element
statement: Given an array nums of size n, return the majority element. The majority element is the element that appears more than n/2 times. The majority element always exists.
hint: Boyer-Moore Voting: maintain a candidate and count; count resets to 0 when it drops below zero.
solution: Initialize candidate=nums[0], count=1. For each element: if count==0 set candidate=element. If element==candidate increment count, else decrement. Return candidate.

name: Longest Consecutive Sequence
statement: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time.
hint: Use a HashSet. Only start counting from numbers where num-1 is NOT in the set.
solution: Put all numbers in a HashSet. For each number, if num-1 is not in set, it's the start of a sequence. Count consecutive numbers starting from there.

name: Move Zeroes
statement: Given an integer array nums, move all 0s to the end while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements. Do it in-place.
hint: Use a slow pointer insertPos for the next non-zero position.
solution: Keep insertPos=0. Iterate with i: when nums[i]!=0, place it at nums[insertPos] and advance insertPos. Fill positions from insertPos to end with 0.

statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.

name: Trapping Rain Water
statement: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
hint: Use two pointers. Water trapped at each position = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i].
solution: Use two pointers l and r. Maintain maxLeft and maxRight. If maxLeft < maxRight, process left side: add maxLeft-height[l] to result, move l right. Otherwise process right side similarly.

name: Container With Most Water
statement: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.
hint: Use two pointers at both ends. Move the pointer with smaller height inward.
solution: Start with pointers at both ends. Area = min(height[l], height[r]) * (r - l). Move the shorter pointer inward. Track maximum area.

statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.

name: Sort Colors
statement: Given an array nums with n objects colored red, white, or blue, represented by 0, 1, and 2 respectively, sort them in-place so that objects of the same color are adjacent, with the colors in the order red, white, and blue.
constraints:
n == nums.length
1 <= n <= 300
nums[i] is either 0, 1, or 2.
hint: Dutch National Flag algorithm with three pointers: low, mid, high.
solution: Low points to next 0 position, high to next 2 position. Move mid: if 0, swap with low; if 2, swap with high; if 1, advance mid.

name: Sliding Window Maximum
statement: You are given an array of integers nums, there is a sliding window of size k which is moving from the very left of the array to the very right. Return the max sliding window.
hint: Use a monotonic deque storing indices.
solution: Use deque. For each element, remove from back if smaller than current. Remove from front if out of window. Front is always the maximum.

name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
statement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
constraints:
0 <= s.length <= 5 * 10^4
s consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces.
hint: Use sliding window with a HashSet to track characters.
solution: Use two pointers l and r. Maintain a set of characters in the window. If s[r] is in the set, remove s[l] and move l right. Otherwise add s[r] and update maxLength.

name: Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K
statement: Given an array of integers and a number k, find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k.
hint: Compute sum of first k elements, then slide window: add next element, remove first element.
solution: Compute windowSum = sum of first k elements. Set maxSum=windowSum. For i=k to n-1: windowSum += arr[i] - arr[i-k]. Update maxSum. Return maxSum.

name: First Negative Number in Every Window
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.

statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.

name: Minimum Window Substring
statement: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string.
constraints:
m == s.length
n == t.length
1 <= m, n <= 10^5
hint: Use sliding window with character frequency counts. Track 'have' vs 'need' count.
solution: Count frequencies in t. Use sliding window on s. Expand right pointer adding chars. When window has all required chars, try shrinking from left. Track minimum window.

name: Product of Array Except Self
statement: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i]. The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and without using the division operation.
hint: Compute prefix products and suffix products separately.
solution: Build a prefix product array and a suffix product array. The answer for index i is prefix[i-1] * suffix[i+1].

name: Subarray Sum Equals K
statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return the total number of subarrays whose sum equals to k.
hint: Use prefix sum. Count of subarrays = count of pairs where prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i] = k.
solution: Maintain running prefix sum and a hashmap of prefix sum counts. For each element, check if (prefixSum - k) exists in map. Add prefixSum to map.

name: Running Sum of 1D Array
statement: Given an array nums, return an array where runningSum[i] = sum of nums[0] through nums[i].
hint: For each i from 1 to n-1, add previous element to current.
solution: Iterate from index 1 to n-1: nums[i] += nums[i-1]. Return modified nums.

name: Longest Subarray with Sum K
statement: Given an array of integers (possibly negative) and integer k, return the length of the longest subarray whose sum equals k.
hint: Use prefix sum + hash map storing first occurrence index of each prefix sum.
solution: Maintain prefixSum and a map of {prefixSum: firstIndex}. For each i, if prefixSum-k exists in map, update maxLen = max(maxLen, i - map[prefixSum-k]). If prefixSum not in map, store it.

name: Equilibrium Index
statement: Find an index in an array such that the sum of elements to its left equals the sum to its right. Return -1 if no such index exists.
hint: totalSum - leftSum - arr[i] = rightSum. Return i when leftSum equals rightSum.
solution: Compute totalSum. Iterate maintaining leftSum. At each index if leftSum == totalSum - leftSum - arr[i], return index. Add arr[i] to leftSum. Return -1.

name: Range Sum Query
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Rotate Array
statement: Given an integer array nums, rotate the array to the right by k steps.
hint: Reverse the whole array, then reverse first k, then reverse remaining n-k.
solution: k = k % n. Reverse entire array. Reverse first k elements. Reverse remaining n-k elements.

name: Leaders in an Array
statement: Given an array, find all leaders. An element is a leader if it is greater than all elements to its right. The rightmost element is always a leader.
hint: Traverse from right maintaining maxRight. If arr[i] > maxRight, it's a leader.
solution: Last element is always leader. Traverse right to left with maxRight. If arr[i] > maxRight, add to result and update maxRight. Reverse result.

name: Maximum Subarray
statement: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.
hint: Kadane's Algorithm: keep a running sum, reset to 0 when negative.
solution: Use Kadane's algorithm. Maintain currentSum and maxSum. If currentSum becomes negative, reset it to 0. Update maxSum at each step.

name: Maximum Product Subarray
statement: Given an integer array nums, find a subarray that has the largest product, and return the product.
hint: Track both max and min product (min can become max when multiplied by negative).
solution: Track curMax and curMin. For each element: newMax = max(num, curMax*num, curMin*num), newMin = min(num, curMax*num, curMin*num). Update global max.

name: Merge Intervals
statement: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.
hint: Sort by start time. Merge if current start <= previous end.
solution: Sort intervals by start. Initialize result with first interval. For each subsequent interval, if start <= result.back().end, merge by updating end to max of both ends. Otherwise add new interval.

name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
statement: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array.
hint: Binary search: the minimum is in the unsorted half.
solution: If nums[mid] > nums[right], minimum is in right half. Otherwise it's in left half (including mid). Continue until left == right.

name: Valid Anagram
statement: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise. An Anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, typically using all the original letters exactly once.
constraints:
1 <= s.length, t.length <= 5 * 10^4
s and t consist of lowercase English letters.
hint: Count character frequencies in both strings and compare.
solution: If lengths differ, return false. Count characters in s (increment) and t (decrement) using an array of size 26. If all counts are 0, they're anagrams.

name: Group Anagrams
statement: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.
constraints:
1 <= strs.length <= 10^4
0 <= strs[i].length <= 100
strs[i] consists of lowercase English letters.
hint: Sort each string as key in a hash map.
solution: For each string, sort it to get a key. Use a hash map where sorted string maps to list of original strings. Return all values.

name: First Unique Character in a String
statement: Given a string s, find the first non-repeating character and return its index. If it does not exist, return -1.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 10^5
s consists of only lowercase English letters.
hint: Count frequencies using array of 26. Return index of first char with count 1.
solution: Count frequency of each character using array[26]. Iterate string again, return index of first character with frequency == 1.

name: Roman to Integer
statement: Given a roman numeral string s, convert it to an integer.
hint: If current value < next value, subtract current; otherwise add current.
solution: Map Roman symbols to values. Traverse string. If value[i] < value[i+1], subtract value[i] from total. Otherwise add value[i].

name: Character Frequency Count
statement: Given a string, find and return the frequency of each character present in the string.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 10^4
s consists of lowercase letters.
hint: Use an array of 26 integers or a HashMap to count occurrences.
solution: Create freq[26] initialized to 0. For each char c in s: freq[c-'a']++. Return map of char to its frequency for all non-zero entries.

name: Count Vowels and Consonants
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Valid Palindrome
statement: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome, or false otherwise.
sampleInput: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 2 * 10^5
s consists only of printable ASCII characters.
hint: Use two pointers, skip non-alphanumeric characters.
solution: Use two pointers l and r. Skip non-alphanumeric characters. Compare lowercase versions of s[l] and s[r]. If mismatch, return false.

name: Reverse String
statement: Write a function that reverses an array of characters in-place using O(1) extra space.
hint: Use two pointers from both ends, swap until they meet in the middle.
solution: Initialize l=0, r=n-1. While l < r: swap s[l] and s[r], l++, r--.

name: Reverse Words in a String
statement: Given an input string s, reverse the order of the words. Remove leading/trailing spaces and reduce multiple spaces to single space.
hint: Trim, split by whitespace, reverse the array, join with single space.
solution: Trim string. Split by one or more whitespace characters. Reverse the words array. Join with single space.

name: String Compression
statement: Compress a character array in-place: consecutive repeating characters are replaced by the character followed by its count (omit count if 1). Return new length.
hint: Use read and write pointers. Count consecutive chars, write char then count digits.
solution: Read pointer i scans, write pointer w writes. Count consecutive same chars. Write char at w. If count>1, write each digit of count. Return w.

name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
statement: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most k times. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 10^5
s consists of only uppercase English letters.
0 <= k <= s.length
hint: Window is valid if (windowLen - maxFreq) <= k.
solution: Use sliding window. Track frequency of each char. If (window_size - max_frequency) > k, shrink window from left. Update maxLen after each step.

name: Find All Anagrams in String
statement: Given two strings s and p, return an array of all the start indices of p's anagrams in s. You may return the answer in any order.
constraints:
1 <= s.length, p.length <= 3 * 10^4
s and p consist of lowercase English letters.
hint: Use fixed-size sliding window of length p.length. Compare character frequencies.
solution: Use frequency arrays for p and current window. Slide window of size p.length across s. Add/remove characters at window boundaries. When frequencies match, add index.

name: Minimum Window Substring
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Longest Common Prefix
statement: Write a function to find the longest common prefix string amongst an array of strings. Return empty string if no common prefix exists.
hint: Compare characters vertically across all strings. Stop at first mismatch.
solution: Use first string as reference. For each character position, check if all strings have same char. Stop when mismatch or any string ends. Return prefix found.

name: Check String Rotation
statement: Given two strings s and goal, return true if s can become goal after some number of left rotations.
hint: s is a rotation of goal if and only if goal is a substring of s+s.
solution: If lengths differ return false. Return whether goal is a substring of (s+s). Use indexOf or KMP.

name: Encode and Decode Strings
statement: Design an algorithm to encode a list of strings to a single string. The encoded string is then sent over the network and is decoded back to the original list of strings.
constraints:
0 <= strs.length < 100
0 <= strs[i].length < 200
strs[i] contains any possible characters out of 256 valid ASCII characters.
hint: Prefix each string with its length and a delimiter.
solution: Encode: for each string, append len(s) + '#' + s. Decode: read length, then '#', then read that many characters.

name: Longest Palindromic Substring
statement: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 1000
s consist of only digits and English letters.
hint: Expand around center. Try each character as center for odd and even length palindromes.
solution: For each index, expand around center for odd length (center at i) and even length (center between i and i+1). Track longest palindrome.

name: Palindromic Substrings
statement: Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it. A string is a palindrome when it reads the same backward as forward. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 1000
s consists of lowercase English letters.
hint: Expand around center. Count each valid palindrome.
solution: For each center (n odd + n-1 even centers), expand while palindrome is valid. Count each valid expansion.

name: Linked List Cycle
statement: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. There is a cycle in a linked list if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer.
constraints:
The number of the nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10^4].
-10^5 <= Node.val <= 10^5
hint: Floyd's cycle detection: slow and fast pointers.
solution: Use slow and fast pointers. Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If they meet, there's a cycle. If fast reaches null, no cycle.

name: Middle of Linked List
statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If two middle nodes, return the second middle.
hint: Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps; when fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
solution: slow=head, fast=head. While fast!=null and fast.next!=null: slow=slow.next, fast=fast.next.next. Return slow.

name: Palindrome Linked List
statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome.
hint: Find middle, reverse the second half, compare both halves.
solution: Find middle with slow/fast pointers. Reverse second half from middle.next. Compare first and reversed second half node by node.

name: Detect Cycle Start in Linked List
statement: Given the head of a linked list, return the node where the cycle begins. If there is no cycle, return null.
constraints:
0 <= number of nodes <= 10^4
pos is -1 or a valid index
hint: After slow and fast meet inside cycle, reset one pointer to head; move both one step at a time.
solution: Floyd's: detect meeting point. Reset slow to head. Move slow and fast one step at a time. Where they meet again is the cycle entry node.

name: Reverse Linked List
statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the list is the range [0, 5000].
-5000 <= Node.val <= 5000
hint: Use prev, curr, next pointers to reverse links iteratively.
solution: Initialize prev=null, curr=head. While curr exists: save next=curr.next, set curr.next=prev, move prev=curr, move curr=next. Return prev.

name: Reverse Linked List in Groups
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
statement: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists in a one sorted list. The list should be made by splicing together the nodes of the first two lists. Return the head of the merged linked list.
constraints:
The number of nodes in both lists is in the range [0, 50].
-100 <= Node.val <= 100
hint: Use a dummy node. Compare heads and attach smaller one.
solution: Create dummy node. Use pointer curr=dummy. Compare list1.val and list2.val. Attach smaller to curr.next. Move that list's pointer and curr forward.

name: Remove Nth Node From End of List
statement: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the list is sz.
1 <= sz <= 30
0 <= Node.val <= 100
1 <= n <= sz
hint: Use two pointers n apart. When fast reaches end, slow is at the node to remove.
solution: Use dummy node. Move fast pointer n+1 steps ahead. Move both slow and fast until fast is null. Remove slow.next.

name: Add Two Numbers
statement: Two non-empty linked lists represent two non-negative integers stored in reverse order. Add the two numbers and return sum as a linked list.
hint: Simulate digit-by-digit addition with carry using a dummy head node.
solution: Use dummy head. Add digits plus carry: sum=val1+val2+carry. New node = sum%10, carry=sum/10. Handle remaining nodes. Add extra node if carry remains.

name: Intersection of Two Linked Lists
statement: Given heads of two singly linked lists, return the node where they intersect. Return null if they don't intersect.
hint: Two pointers each traverse both lists; they meet at intersection after m+n steps.
solution: a=headA, b=headB. Advance both. When a==null redirect to headB; when b==null redirect to headA. They meet at intersection or both null.

name: Find The Duplicate Number
statement: Given an array of integers nums containing n + 1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive. There is only one repeated number in nums, return this repeated number.
hint: Treat array as linked list. Use Floyd's cycle detection.
solution: Use Floyd's algorithm treating each value as pointer to index. Find cycle entry point which is the duplicate.

name: Valid Parentheses
statement: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets and in the correct order.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 10^4
s consists of parentheses only '()[]{}'.
hint: Push opening brackets. On closing bracket, check if top matches.
solution: Use a stack. For '(', '[', '{', push. For ')', ']', '}', if stack empty or top doesn't match, return false. Pop otherwise. Return stack.empty() at end.

name: Min Stack
statement: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.
hint: Maintain two stacks: main stack and a min-tracking stack.
solution: Main stack stores all values. Min stack stores minimum at each level. On push: push to both (min stack gets min of new val and current min). On pop: pop from both.

name: Implement Queue Using Stacks
statement: Implement a first in first out (FIFO) queue using only two stacks. The implemented queue should support push, pop, peek, and empty operations.
constraints:
1 <= x <= 9
At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, peek, and empty.
hint: Use two stacks: input and output. Transfer when output is empty.
solution: Maintain two stacks. Push to stack1. Pop: if stack2 empty, transfer all from stack1 to stack2, then pop from stack2.

name: Daily Temperatures
statement: Given an array of integers temperatures represents the daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature.
hint: Use a monotonic decreasing stack storing indices.
solution: Use a stack of indices. For each temperature, pop indices from stack while current temp is greater than stack top temp. Set answer[popped] = current_idx - popped_idx.

name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
statement: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram's bar height where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.
hint: Use monotonic increasing stack. Pop when current height is smaller.
solution: Use stack of (index, height). When popping, calculate area using popped height and current width. Extend start position. Process remaining stack at the end.

name: Next Greater Element
statement: For each element in nums1 (subset of nums2), find the next greater element to its right in nums2. Return -1 if none exists.
constraints:
1 <= nums1.length <= nums2.length <= 1000
All integers are unique
hint: Use monotonic decreasing stack on nums2 to map each element to its next greater.
solution: Process nums2 with monotonic stack. When larger element found, pop stack and map popped→current. For nums1 elements, return map value or -1.

name: Stock Span Problem
statement: The span of a stock price today is the maximum number of consecutive days (including today) the price was less than or equal to today's price. Return span for each day.
hint: Stack stores (price, span) pairs. Pop while top price <= current, accumulate spans.
solution: For each price: span=1. While stack not empty and top.price <= current price: span += top.span, pop. Push (price, span). Return span.

name: Sliding Window Maximum
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Design Circular Queue
statement: Design a circular queue of size k with enQueue, deQueue, Front, Rear, isEmpty, isFull operations.
hint: Use array of size k with front and rear pointers. Use modular arithmetic for wrap-around.
solution: Array[k], front=0, rear=-1, count=0. enQueue: if full return false; rear=(rear+1)%k; array[rear]=val; count++. deQueue: if empty return false; front=(front+1)%k; count--.

name: First Non-Repeating Character in Stream
statement: Given a stream of characters, at each point print the first non-repeating character. Print '#' if no such character exists.
hint: Use frequency array and a queue of candidates. Pop front when its frequency > 1.
solution: freq[26] array + queue. For each char: increment freq, enqueue char. While queue front has freq>1, dequeue. Print front or '#' if empty.

name: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. A binary tree's maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4].
-100 <= Node.val <= 100
hint: Return 1 + max(depth of left, depth of right).
solution: If root is null, return 0. Return 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right)).

name: Invert Binary Tree
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return its root.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100].
-100 <= Node.val <= 100
hint: Recursively swap left and right children.
solution: Recursively call invertTree on left and right subtrees. Swap root.left and root.right. Return root.

name: Diameter of Binary Tree
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the length of the diameter of the tree. The diameter of a binary tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes in a tree.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4].
hint: For each node, diameter through it = leftDepth + rightDepth.
solution: DFS returns height. At each node, update global maxDiameter = max(maxDiameter, leftH + rightH). Return 1 + max(leftH, rightH).

name: Balanced Binary Tree
statement: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced. A height-balanced binary tree is a binary tree in which the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than one.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 5000].
hint: Return -1 if unbalanced. Otherwise return height.
solution: DFS returns height or -1 if unbalanced. At each node: if either subtree returns -1, or |leftH-rightH| > 1, return -1. Else return 1 + max(leftH, rightH).

name: Path Sum
statement: Given the root of a binary tree and an integer targetSum, return true if the tree has a root-to-leaf path such that adding up all the values along the path equals targetSum.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 5000].
-1000 <= Node.val <= 1000
-1000 <= targetSum <= 1000
hint: DFS. At leaf, check if remaining == node.val.
solution: DFS with remaining target. At each node, subtract node.val from target. At leaf, return target == 0.

name: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder
statement: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.
hint: Preorder first element is root. Find it in inorder to split left/right subtrees.
solution: Root is preorder[0]. Find its index in inorder. Elements to left are left subtree, right are right subtree. Recursively build.

name: LCA of Binary Tree
statement: Given a binary tree (not BST) and two nodes p and q, find the Lowest Common Ancestor. The LCA is the deepest node that has both p and q as descendants.
hint: If root is p or q return root. Recurse left and right. If both non-null, root is LCA.
solution: If root==null or root==p or root==q return root. left=lca(left,p,q); right=lca(right,p,q). If both non-null return root. Return whichever is non-null.

name: Validate Binary Search Tree
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4].
-2^31 <= Node.val <= 2^31 - 1
hint: Pass min and max bounds. Check if node.val is within bounds.
solution: Recursive helper with min/max bounds. For left subtree, max = node.val. For right subtree, min = node.val. If node.val not in (min, max), return false.

name: Lowest Common Ancestor of BST
statement: Given a binary search tree (BST), find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) node of two given nodes in the BST.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [2, 10^5].
-10^9 <= Node.val <= 10^9
hint: Use BST property: if both p,q < root, go left. If both > root, go right. Otherwise root is LCA.
solution: If p.val and q.val < root.val, recurse left. If both > root.val, recurse right. Otherwise, return root.

name: Kth Smallest in BST
statement: Given the root of a binary search tree, and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) of all the values of the nodes in the tree.
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is n.
1 <= k <= n <= 10^4
0 <= Node.val <= 10^4
hint: In-order traversal of BST gives sorted order. Return kth element.
solution: In-order DFS (left, root, right). Decrement k at each node. When k==0, record and return the value.

name: Search in a BST
statement: Given root of a BST and an integer val, find the node whose value equals val and return the subtree rooted at it. Return null if not found.
hint: Use BST property: if val < root go left, if val > root go right.
solution: If root==null or root.val==val return root. If val < root.val recurse left else recurse right.

name: Insert into a BST
statement: Given root of a BST and a value to insert (guaranteed not to exist), return the root after insertion.
constraints:
0 <= node count <= 10^4
All values are unique
hint: Traverse using BST property; insert new node at the null leaf position.
solution: If root==null create and return new node. If val < root.val recurse left. Else recurse right. Return root.

name: Level Order Traversal
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 2000].
-1000 <= Node.val <= 1000
hint: Use a queue. Process nodes level by level.
solution: Use BFS with a queue. For each level, record its size, process exactly that many nodes, add their children to queue. Append level values to result.

name: Right Side View of Binary Tree
statement: Given the root of a binary tree, imagine yourself standing on the right side of it, return the values of the nodes you can see ordered from top to bottom.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100].
hint: BFS level order. Take the last element of each level.
solution: BFS level order traversal. At each level, the last node processed is visible from right side. Add it to result.

name: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
statement: Given root of a binary tree, return zigzag level order traversal: left to right, then right to left, alternating.
hint: BFS level order; toggle a direction flag per level and reverse accordingly.
solution: BFS with queue. For each level collect values. If level index is odd, reverse the values array before adding to result.

name: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
statement: Serialization is the process of converting a data structure or object into a sequence of bits so that it can be stored in a file or memory buffer. Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4].
hint: Use BFS or DFS. Serialize null nodes explicitly.
solution: BFS serialize: level order, write 'null' for missing nodes. Deserialize: BFS, connect nodes to their parent using queue.

name: Bubble Sort
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.

name: Merge Sort
statement: Given an array of integers nums, sort the array in ascending order and return it. You must solve the problem without using any built-in functions in O(n log n) time complexity and with the smallest space complexity possible.
hint: Divide array into halves, recursively sort, then merge sorted halves.
solution: Recursively divide array into halves. Merge two sorted halves using auxiliary array by comparing elements one by one.

name: Quick Sort
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.

name: Sort Colors
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.

sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Kth Largest Element in Array
statement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest element in the sorted order, not the kth distinct element.
hint: Use a min-heap of size k. Maintain k largest elements.
solution: Use a min-heap of size k. For each element, push to heap. If heap size > k, pop minimum. At end, top of heap is kth largest.

name: Binary Search
statement: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, then return its index. Otherwise, return -1.
constraints:
1 <= nums.length <= 10^4
-10^4 < nums[i], target < 10^4
All the integers in nums are unique.
nums is sorted in ascending order.
hint: Classic binary search with left and right pointers.
solution: Set l=0, r=n-1. While l<=r, mid=(l+r)/2. If nums[mid]==target return mid. If nums[mid]<target, l=mid+1. Else r=mid-1.

name: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
statement: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order (with distinct values). Given the array nums after the possible rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums.
constraints:
1 <= nums.length <= 5000
-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4
All values of nums are unique.
nums is an ascending array that is possibly rotated.
-10^4 <= target <= 10^4
hint: Determine which half is sorted. Check if target lies in the sorted half.
solution: At each step, one half must be sorted. If left half is sorted (nums[l]<=nums[mid]) and target is in that range, search left. Otherwise search right. Vice versa for right half.

name: Find Peak Element
statement: A peak element is strictly greater than its neighbors. Given an array, find any peak element index. Adjacent elements are not equal.
hint: If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], peak must be to the right; else to the left.
solution: Binary search: if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], set l=mid+1 else r=mid. Loop while l<r. Return l.

name: Search Insert Position
statement: Given a sorted array of distinct integers and a target, return the index if found. If not, return the index where it would be inserted in order.
hint: Standard binary search; when not found, the left pointer is the insertion position.
solution: Binary search with l=0, r=n-1. When target found return mid. When l>r, return l (insertion point).

name: First and Last Position of Element
statement: Given a sorted array and target, find the starting and ending position of target. If not found, return [-1, -1].
hint: Run binary search twice: once biased left for first occurrence, once biased right for last.
solution: First: binary search, when found continue searching left (r=mid-1). Last: binary search, when found continue searching right (l=mid+1). Return both positions.

name: Square Root using Binary Search
statement: Given a non-negative integer x, return the floor of its square root without using sqrt().
hint: Binary search from 1 to x. Find largest mid where mid*mid <= x.
solution: If x<2 return x. Binary search l=1, r=x/2. If mid*mid==x return mid. If mid*mid<x, ans=mid, l=mid+1. Else r=mid-1. Return ans.

name: Koko Eating Bananas
statement: Koko loves to eat bananas. There are n piles of bananas, the ith pile has piles[i] bananas. The guards have gone and will come back in h hours. Koko can decide her bananas-per-hour eating speed of k. Each hour, she chooses some pile of bananas and eats k bananas from that pile. Return the minimum integer k such that she can eat all the bananas within h hours.
hint: Binary search on the answer (speed k). Check if given speed k can finish in h hours.
solution: Binary search between 1 and max(piles). For each mid speed, calculate total hours needed (ceil(pile/mid) for each pile). If hours <= h, try smaller speed.

name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Search a 2D Matrix
statement: Given m×n matrix where each row is sorted and each row's first element > previous row's last element, efficiently search for target.
hint: Treat 2D matrix as 1D sorted array. Virtual index mid maps to matrix[mid/n][mid%n].
solution: lo=0, hi=m*n-1. While lo<=hi: mid=(lo+hi)/2; val=matrix[mid/n][mid%n]. If val==target return true. If val<target lo=mid+1 else hi=mid-1. Return false.

name: Jump Game
statement: You are given an integer array nums. You are initially positioned at the array's first index, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position. Return true if you can reach the last index, or false otherwise.
hint: Track the maximum reachable index. If current index > maxReach, return false.
solution: Maintain maxReach. For each index i, if i > maxReach, return false. Update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]). If maxReach >= n-1, return true.

name: Jump Game II
statement: Given an array of non-negative integers where each element is max jump length, return the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index.
hint: Track current jump end and farthest reachable. Jump when reaching current end.
solution: jumps=0, currentEnd=0, farthest=0. For each i (except last): farthest=max(farthest,i+nums[i]). If i==currentEnd: jumps++, currentEnd=farthest. Return jumps.

name: Gas Station
statement: There are n gas stations along a circular route. You are given two integer arrays gas and cost. Return the starting gas station's index if you can travel around the circuit once in the clockwise direction, otherwise return -1.
constraints:
n == gas.length == cost.length
1 <= n <= 10^5
0 <= gas[i], cost[i] <= 10^4
hint: If total gas >= total cost, a solution exists. Start from the station after deficit.
solution: If total gas < total cost, no solution. Otherwise, find starting point by tracking running tank. When tank goes negative, reset start to next station.

name: Max Profit II (Multiple Transactions)
statement: You are given an integer array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. On each day, you may decide to buy and/or sell the stock. You can only hold at most one share of the stock at any time. Find and return the maximum profit you can achieve.
hint: Add all positive differences between consecutive days.
solution: Sum all positive differences: for each consecutive pair, if prices[i] > prices[i-1], add the difference to profit.

name: Assign Cookies
statement: Given greed factors g[] and cookie sizes s[], maximize the number of content children. A child is content if cookie size >= greed factor.
hint: Sort both arrays. Use two pointers: if cookie >= greed, satisfy child and advance both.
solution: Sort g and s. i=0 (children), j=0 (cookies). While both valid: if s[j]>=g[i] increment i (satisfied). Always increment j. Return i.

name: Non-Overlapping Intervals
statement: Given an array of intervals, return the minimum number of intervals to remove to make the rest non-overlapping.
hint: Sort by end time. Greedily keep intervals with earliest end. Count removed.
solution: Sort by end. prevEnd=intervals[0][1], count=0. For i=1 to n-1: if start>=prevEnd update prevEnd. Else count++ (remove current, keep smaller end). Return count.

name: Activity Selection Problem
statement: Given n activities with start and end times, select the maximum number of non-overlapping activities that can be performed by a single person.
hint: Sort by end time. Greedily select activity if start >= lastEnd.
solution: Sort activities by end time. Select first, count=1, lastEnd=end[0]. For each i>0: if start[i]>=lastEnd, count++, lastEnd=end[i]. Return count.

name: Job Sequencing Problem
statement: Given jobs with deadlines and profits, find the sequence of jobs that maximizes total profit. Each job takes 1 unit of time and must be done before its deadline.
hint: Sort by profit descending. For each job, find latest free slot before deadline.
solution: Sort by profit desc. Boolean slots array. For each job: find latest free slot from min(deadline,maxD) down to 1. Schedule if slot found.

name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
statement: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock. Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction. If you cannot achieve any profit, return 0.
hint: Track the minimum price seen so far and the maximum profit.
solution: Iterate through the array keeping track of the minimum price seen so far. For each price, calculate profit = price - minPrice, and update maxProfit if it's greater.

name: Minimum Number of Coins
statement: Solve the problem efficiently using an appropriate algorithm.
sampleInput: See LeetCode / competitive programming platform for examples.
sampleOutput: Output depends on the specific input.
hint: Think about the time and space complexity. Consider edge cases.
solution: Apply the pattern described in the pattern field.

name: Climbing Stairs
statement: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?
hint: This is Fibonacci. ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2).
solution: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Base cases dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2. Or use two variables for O(1) space.

name: House Robber
statement: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. You cannot rob two adjacent houses. Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.
hint: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).
solution: For each house, either skip it (take dp[i-1]) or rob it (dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Use two variables prev1 and prev2.

name: Fibonacci Number
statement: The Fibonacci numbers form a sequence, called the Fibonacci sequence, such that each number is the sum of the two preceding ones, starting from 0 and 1. Given n, calculate F(n).
hint: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), base cases F(0)=0, F(1)=1.
solution: Use memoization with recursion. Or iterative: keep track of previous two values.

name: N-th Tribonacci Number
statement: The Tribonacci sequence Tn is defined as: T0=0, T1=1, T2=1, and Tn+3 = Tn + Tn+1 + Tn+2 for n >= 0. Given n, return the value of Tn.
hint: Similar to Fibonacci but uses 3 previous values.
solution: Track three variables t0, t1, t2. Each step: next = t0+t1+t2, shift values. Return t2 after n steps.

name: Decode Ways
statement: A message containing letters from A-Z can be encoded into numbers using A->1, B->2, ..., Z->26. Given a string s containing only digits, return the number of ways to decode it.
hint: Similar to Fibonacci. Check if 1-digit and 2-digit substrings form valid letters.
solution: Use prev1 and prev2. If s[i] != '0', add prev1. If s[i-1..i] is between '10' and '26', add prev2. Shift variables.

name: Word Break
statement: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of dictionary words.
hint: dp[i] = true if s[0..i-1] can be segmented. Check all valid substrings ending at i.
solution: dp[0] = true. For each i from 1 to len: check all j < i where dp[j] is true. If s[j..i-1] is in dict, set dp[i]=true and break inner loop.

name: Longest Increasing Subsequence
statement: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
hint: O(n log n): maintain tails array. Binary search for position to replace.
solution: Maintain tails array. For each num, binary search for first tail >= num. Replace it. If num > all tails, extend. tails.length is answer.

name: Coin Change
statement: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount. If that amount of money cannot be made up by any combination of the coins, return -1.
hint: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i. dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1) for each coin <= i.
solution: Bottom-up DP: dp[0]=0, dp[i]=amount+1 (sentinel) for i>0. For each amount i from 1 to target, try every coin <= i and take dp[i]=min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1). Return dp[amount] if <= amount else -1.

name: Longest Common Subsequence
statement: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. If there is no common subsequence, return 0.
hint: dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[0..i] and text2[0..j].
solution: 2D DP table. If text1[i]==text2[j], dp[i][j]=1+dp[i-1][j-1]. Else dp[i][j]=max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

name: 0-1 Knapsack
statement: Given weights and values of n items, put these items in a knapsack of capacity W to get the maximum total value. You cannot break items (0/1 property).
hint: dp[w] = max value using items processed so far with capacity w.
solution: Use a 1D DP array of size W+1. For each item, iterate backwards from W to item weight: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight] + value).

name: Minimum Path Sum
statement: Given an m x n grid filled with non-negative numbers, find a path from top left to bottom right, which minimizes the sum of all numbers along its path. You can only move either down or right at any point in time.
constraints:
m == grid.length
n == grid[i].length
1 <= m, n <= 200
0 <= grid[i][j] <= 200
hint: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
solution: Build a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] is the min path sum to reach cell (i,j). First row/column can only be reached one way (sum of prefix). For other cells, dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Return dp[m-1][n-1].

name: Unique Paths
statement: There is a robot on an m x n grid. The robot can only move either down or right. Return the number of possible unique paths to reach the bottom-right corner.
solution: Maintain a 1D DP array for the current row. For each cell, dp[j] += dp[j-1]. The last element is the answer.

name: Edit Distance
statement: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace) required to convert word1 to word2.
hint: dp[i][j] = min(insert, delete, replace) + 1 if characters differ.
solution: Fill DP matrix. If chars match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1.

name: Partition Equal Subset Sum
statement: Given an integer array nums, return true if you can partition it into two subsets with equal sum.
hint: If total sum is odd, return false. Find subset with sum = total/2.
solution: target=totalSum/2. 1D DP boolean array of size target+1. For num in nums, iterate backwards: dp[j] = dp[j] || dp[j-num].

name: Burst Balloons
statement: You are given n balloons, indexed from 0 to n-1. Each balloon is painted with a number on it represented by an array nums. You are asked to burst all the balloons. If you burst balloon i, you get nums[i-1] * nums[i] * nums[i+1] coins (using 1 as the value for out-of-bounds neighbors). Return the maximum coins you can collect by bursting the balloons wisely.
hint: Think in reverse: instead of which balloon to burst first, decide which balloon to burst LAST in each interval.
solution: Pad nums with 1 on both ends. dp[l][r] = max coins from bursting all balloons strictly between l and r (exclusive), where l and r are never burst within this subproblem. For each k between l and r as the LAST balloon burst in that range: dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], dp[l][k-1] + nums[l-1]*nums[k]*nums[r+1] + dp[k+1][r]). Answer is dp[1][n] on the padded array.

name: Maximum Subarray
statement: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.
hint: Kadane's Algorithm: keep a running sum, reset to 0 when negative.
solution: Use Kadane's algorithm. Maintain currentSum and maxSum. If currentSum becomes negative, reset it to 0. Update maxSum at each step.

name: Maximum Product Subarray
statement: Given an integer array nums, find a subarray that has the largest product, and return the product.
hint: Keep track of both the maximum and minimum products ending at the current element.
solution: Track curMax and curMin. If num is negative, swap curMax and curMin. curMax = max(num, curMax*num), curMin = min(num, curMin*num).

name: BFS Shortest Path
statement: Given an unweighted, undirected graph with n nodes labeled 0 to n-1 and a list of edges, find the length of the shortest path (in number of edges) between src and dest. Return -1 if no path exists.
hint: BFS from src naturally finds shortest paths in an unweighted graph, since it explores nodes in increasing order of distance.
solution: Build an adjacency list from the edges. Run BFS starting at src, tracking distance for each visited node. Return dist[dest] once dequeued, or -1 if BFS finishes without reaching dest.

name: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
statement: Given a matrix of heights, find all coordinates where water can flow to BOTH the Pacific (top/left) and Atlantic (bottom/right) oceans.
hint: Work backwards. Start from the oceans and traverse UP the heights to see which cells can reach each ocean.
solution: Run DFS/BFS from Pacific border cells and Atlantic border cells. Track reachable cells in two boolean grids. Intersection is the answer.

name: Walls and Gates
statement: You are given an m x n grid rooms initialized with -1 (a wall), 0 (a gate), or INF = 2147483647 (an empty room). Fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate. If it is impossible to reach a gate, the room should remain INF.
constraints:
m == rooms.length
n == rooms[i].length
1 <= m, n <= 250
rooms[i][j] is -1, 0, or 2^31 - 1
hint: Start BFS simultaneously from ALL gates at once (multi-source BFS) instead of running BFS from every empty room.
solution: Push all gate cells (value 0) into a queue. Run BFS from all of them simultaneously; whenever we reach an INF cell, set its distance as current distance + 1 and push it. Because BFS explores in order of distance, the first time a cell is reached gives its shortest distance to any gate.

name: Rotting Oranges
statement: In a grid, 1 is fresh, 2 is rotten. Every minute, fresh oranges adjacent to rotten become rotten. Return minimum minutes until no fresh remain, or -1.
hint: Queue all initially rotten oranges. Track count of fresh oranges. Perform layer-by-level BFS.
solution: Push all '2's into Queue. Count '1's. Loop Queue layer by layer (minutes). Rots neighbors, decrement fresh count. Return time if fresh == 0 else -1.

name: Word Ladder
statement: Find length of shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord changing exactly one letter at a time, with all intermediate words in wordList.
hint: Shortest path in an unweighted graph -> BFS. Generate neighbors by changing characters a-z.
solution: Convert wordList to Set. BFS queue stores (word, steps). Try changing each character from 'a'-'z'. If in Set, remove from Set and queue.

name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
statement: Given a weighted, directed graph with n nodes and a list of edges [u, v, weight], find the shortest distance from a source node src to every other node using Dijkstra's algorithm. Return an array of shortest distances.
hint: Standard Dijkstra's using a Min-Priority Queue.
solution: Min-Heap storing (distance, node). Start with (0, src). Pop, if unvisited, mark and relax all neighbors. Max value in distances array is answer.

name: Number of Islands
statement: Given an m x n 2D binary grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands.
hint: Iterate through the grid. When a '1' is found, run BFS/DFS to mark the entire island as visited.
solution: Scan cells. On '1', increment island count and trigger DFS/BFS to zero out all connected '1's recursively.

name: Clone Graph
statement: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone) of the graph.
hint: Use a HashMap to store the mapping from original nodes to cloned nodes to avoid infinite cycles.
solution: DFS traversal. If node is in map, return clone. Else, create clone, put in map, then recursively clone neighbors and append.

name: Course Schedule
statement: There are numCourses courses. You are given an array prerequisites. Return true if you can finish all courses, false if there is a cycle.
hint: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. Use Kahn's algorithm or DFS with states.
solution: Calculate indegrees. Push nodes with indegree 0 to a queue. For each popped node, decrement neighbors' indegrees and queue if 0. Valid if processed count == numCourses.

name: Detect Cycle in Directed Graph
statement: Given a directed graph with V vertices and an adjacency list adj, determine if the graph contains a cycle.
hint: Track 3 states per node: unvisited, in the current DFS recursion stack, and fully processed. A back-edge to a node in the current stack means a cycle.
solution: DFS with a state array (0=unvisited, 1=visiting/in-stack, 2=done). For a node u, mark state[u]=1, recurse into neighbors; if a neighbor is state 1, a cycle is found. After processing all neighbors, mark state[u]=2. Try DFS from every unvisited node.

name: Flood Fill
statement: You are given an image represented by an m x n grid of integers, a starting pixel (sr, sc), and a new color. Perform a flood fill: starting from the given pixel, change the color of that pixel and all connected pixels (4-directionally) of the same original color to the new color. Return the modified image.
constraints:
m == image.length
n == image[i].length
1 <= m, n <= 50
0 <= image[i][j], color < 2^16
hint: Standard DFS/BFS flood fill; remember to check the new color equals the old color to avoid infinite recursion.
solution: If the starting pixel's color already equals the new color, return the image unchanged (prevents infinite loop). Otherwise DFS/BFS from (sr,sc), recoloring every 4-directionally connected pixel that matches the original color.

name: Word Search
statement: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically neighboring), and the same cell may not be used more than once.
hint: DFS from every cell matching word[0]; temporarily mark visited cells so they aren't reused, then restore them on backtrack.
solution: For each cell matching word[0], run DFS: mark the cell visited (e.g. overwrite with a sentinel), recurse into the 4 neighbors looking for the next character, and restore the cell after exploring. Return true as soon as any starting cell completes the full word.

name: Alien Dictionary
statement: There is a new alien language which uses the English alphabet, but the order among letters is unknown. You are given a list of words from the alien dictionary, sorted lexicographically by the rules of this new language. Derive the order of letters in this language, or return an empty string if the order is invalid.
hint: Compare each pair of adjacent words to find the first differing character; that gives a 'comes before' edge. Then topologically sort the resulting graph.
solution: Build a graph over the distinct letters. For every pair of consecutive words, find the first index where they differ and add an edge from that char in word1 to the char in word2 (invalid if word1 is longer than word2 but a prefix of it). Run Kahn's BFS topological sort; if all letters are ordered, return the order, otherwise return empty string (cycle detected).

name: Course Schedule II
statement: Given numCourses and prerequisites, return the ordering of courses you should take to finish all courses. Return empty array if impossible.
hint: Use Kahn's algorithm. Store nodes popped from the 0-indegree queue.
solution: Build adjacency list and indegree array. Queue 0-indegree nodes. Append popped nodes to result. If result size != numCourses, return [].

name: Number of Connected Components
statement: Given n nodes and an array of undirected edges, return the number of connected components in the graph.
hint: Use Union Find. Start with n components and decrement on each successful union.
solution: Initialize parent array. Start count = n. Iterate edges, perform union. If nodes were not already connected, decrement count.

name: Redundant Connection
statement: In this problem, a tree is an undirected graph that is connected and has no cycles. You are given a graph that started as a tree with n nodes, with one additional edge added, creating exactly one cycle. Return the edge that can be removed so that the resulting graph is a tree of n nodes. If there are multiple answers, return the answer that occurs last in the input.
constraints:
n == edges.length
3 <= n <= 1000
1 <= edges[i][0] < edges[i][1] <= n
hint: Process edges in order, using Union-Find; the first edge that connects two nodes already in the same component is the redundant one.
solution: Use Union-Find with path compression. For each edge (u,v) in order, if find(u)==find(v), u and v are already connected, so this edge creates the cycle — return it immediately. Otherwise union them.

name: Fibonacci Number
statement: The Fibonacci numbers form a sequence, called the Fibonacci sequence, such that each number is the sum of the two preceding ones, starting from 0 and 1. Given n, calculate F(n).
hint: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), base cases F(0)=0, F(1)=1.
solution: Use memoization with recursion. Or iterative: keep track of previous two values.

name: Factorial using Recursion
statement: Given a non-negative integer n, compute n! (n factorial) using recursion.
hint: Base case: factorial(0) = 1. Recursive case: factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1).
solution: If n <= 1, return 1. Otherwise return n * factorial(n-1). Each call reduces n by 1 until the base case is hit.

name: Sum of Digits using Recursion
statement: Given a non-negative integer n, compute the sum of its digits using recursion.
hint: Base case: sumOfDigits(0) = 0. Recursive case: sumOfDigits(n) = n%10 + sumOfDigits(n/10).
solution: If n == 0, return 0. Otherwise return (n % 10) + sumOfDigits(n / 10), peeling off the last digit each call.

name: Power Function (Fast Exponentiation)
statement: Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n (i.e., x^n).
hint: If n is even, x^n = (x^2)^(n/2). Handle negative n carefully to avoid integer overflow.
solution: Long absN = abs(n). ans = 1.0. While absN > 0: if absN is odd, ans *= x. x *= x, absN >>= 1. If n < 0 return 1/ans.

name: Tower of Hanoi
statement: Given n disks stacked on one peg (largest at bottom), and two other empty pegs, return the minimum number of moves required to move all disks to another peg, following the rules: only one disk can be moved at a time, and a larger disk may never be placed on top of a smaller disk.
hint: To move n disks: move the top n-1 disks to the auxiliary peg, move the largest disk to the destination, then move the n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination.
solution: The minimum number of moves follows the recurrence T(n) = 2*T(n-1) + 1 with T(0)=0, which solves to T(n) = 2^n - 1. This can be computed directly or via the same recursive move-count logic.

name: Subsets
statement: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets.
constraints:
1 <= nums.length <= 10
-10 <= nums[i] <= 10
All elements are unique
hint: At each index, branch into two choices: include the current element or skip it (standard backtracking template).
solution: Backtrack starting at index 0 with an empty running subset: add the current subset to the result at every recursive call, then for each index i from start to n-1, include nums[i], recurse with start=i+1, then remove nums[i] (backtrack).

name: Permutations
statement: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all the possible permutations. You can return the answer in any order.
hint: Swap-based backtracking: at each recursion depth, try placing each unused remaining element in the current position.
solution: Use std::next_permutation on a sorted copy of nums, or classic backtracking: track a boolean 'used' array, build the current permutation, and at each step try every unused number, recurse, then mark unused again on backtrack.

name: Combination Sum
statement: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return a list of all unique combinations of candidates where the chosen numbers sum to target. The same number may be chosen from candidates an unlimited number of times.
hint: Sort candidates first so you can prune early once the remaining target goes negative. Reuse the same index (not index+1) to allow repeats.
solution: Backtrack with (start index, remaining target). At each step, try every candidate from start to end; if candidates[i] > remaining, break (sorted order allows pruning). Otherwise include candidates[i], recurse with the SAME start=i (allows reuse) and remaining -= candidates[i]; then backtrack.

name: Letter Combinations of Phone Number
statement: Given a string containing digits from 2-9, return all possible letter combinations that the number could represent, using the standard telephone keypad letter mapping. Return the answer in any order.
constraints:
0 <= digits.length <= 4
digits[i] is a digit in the range ['2', '9']
hint: Map each digit to its letters, then backtrack building the combination one digit at a time.
solution: If digits is empty, return an empty list. Use a digit-to-letters lookup table. Backtrack: at recursion depth idx, try every letter mapped to digits[idx], append it to the current combination, recurse to idx+1, then remove it. Base case: idx == digits.length adds the combination to the result.

name: N-Queens
statement: The n-queens puzzle is the problem of placing n queens on an n x n chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Given an integer n, return all distinct solutions to the n-queens puzzle.
hint: Place one queen per row. Track used columns and both diagonals (row+col and row-col) to check attacks in O(1).
solution: Backtrack row by row. For each row, try every column; check if the column, and both diagonals, are free using boolean arrays. If free, place the queen, mark the column/diagonals used, recurse to the next row, then unmark on backtrack. When row == n, record the board configuration.

name: Word Search
statement: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically neighboring), and the same cell may not be used more than once. (Revision repeat of Word Search.)
hint: DFS from every cell matching word[0]; temporarily mark visited cells so they aren't reused, then restore them on backtrack.
solution: For each cell matching word[0], run DFS: mark the cell visited (e.g. overwrite with a sentinel), recurse into the 4 neighbors looking for the next character, and restore the cell after exploring. Return true as soon as any starting cell completes the full word.

name: Kth Largest Element in Array
statement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest element in the sorted order, not the kth distinct element.
hint: Use a min-heap of size k. Maintain k largest elements.
solution: Use a min-heap of size k. For each element, push to heap. If heap size > k, pop minimum. At end, top of heap is kth largest.

name: Find Median from Data Stream
statement: Design a data structure that supports adding integer numbers from a data stream and finding the median of the numbers so far.
hint: Maintain the lower half of numbers in a max-heap and the upper half in a min-heap.
solution: Max-heap (lo) and Min-heap (hi). Push to lo, pop max from lo to hi. Balance: if hi size > lo size, pop min from hi to lo. Median is top of lo, or avg(lo, hi).

name: Merge K Sorted Lists
statement: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.
hint: Use a min-heap holding the current head of each list; repeatedly pop the smallest and push its successor.
solution: Push the head of every non-null list into a min-priority-queue keyed by node value. Repeatedly pop the smallest node, append it to the result list, and if that node has a next, push the next node. Continue until the heap is empty.

name: Top K Frequent Elements
statement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements in any order.
hint: Count frequencies using a map. Maintain a min-heap of size k based on frequencies.
solution: Map frequencies. Push pairs into Min-Heap. If heap size exceeds k, pop. Elements remaining in heap are the answer. (Bucket sort is O(n)).
